profile.overview: NVIDIA is a public semiconductor and accelerated-computing company founded in 1993 and headquartered in Santa Clara, California. It designs GPUs, AI systems, networking products, and software platforms used across data centers, gaming, automotive, and robotics markets. Public-company materials and market data sources report a workforce of more than 30,000 employees. Its strategy centers on selling a full-stack AI infrastructure platform rather than individual chips alone.
profile.products_and_services: NVIDIA's major offerings include GeForce gaming GPUs, RTX professional graphics, data-center GPUs, DGX and HGX systems, InfiniBand and Ethernet networking, and the CUDA software ecosystem. Its core customers include hyperscalers, enterprises building AI workloads, OEM partners, research institutions, and developers. The company's value proposition is that it combines hardware, systems, networking, and software into an integrated accelerated-computing platform.

financial.financial_snapshot: NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) has a deterministic financial snapshot sourced from structured yfinance fields. Revenue is $130.5B, revenue growth is 114.0%, profit margin is 55.9%, operating margin is 61.1%, gross margin is 75.0%, and full-time employee count is unavailable. Any unavailable field is left unavailable rather than estimated or fabricated.

competitors.name: AMD
competitors.why_it_competes: AMD competes with NVIDIA in AI accelerators, data-center GPUs, and supporting software platforms for enterprise and hyperscaler workloads.
competitors.differentiator: AMD's differentiator is its broader CPU-plus-GPU portfolio and its push to position Instinct accelerators as a more open alternative in certain enterprise environments.

competitors.name: Intel
competitors.why_it_competes: Intel competes with NVIDIA in data-center compute, AI accelerators, and enterprise infrastructure budgets.
competitors.differentiator: Intel's key differentiator is its large installed base in server CPUs and long-standing enterprise procurement relationships.

competitors.name: Broadcom
competitors.why_it_competes: Broadcom competes in AI infrastructure through custom silicon and networking, especially for hyperscaler-led alternatives to standard GPU-heavy stacks.
competitors.differentiator: Broadcom is differentiated by its strength in custom silicon and high-performance connectivity for tailored AI infrastructure.

recent_news.headline: NVIDIA reported record fiscal 2026 revenue driven by data-center demand
recent_news.summary: In its fourth-quarter and fiscal 2026 results, NVIDIA said revenue rose sharply year over year, reinforcing the scale of demand for AI infrastructure and the centrality of its data-center business.

recent_news.headline: NVIDIA unveiled Blackwell Ultra and next-generation AI infrastructure at GTC 2026
recent_news.summary: At GTC 2026, NVIDIA introduced Blackwell Ultra systems and related platform updates, signaling continued product cadence and an effort to preserve performance leadership in AI infrastructure.

recent_news.headline: Export controls remained a strategic variable for NVIDIA's China business
recent_news.summary: Policy restrictions on advanced AI chip exports continued to shape how NVIDIA serves China, making regulation a material factor in revenue mix and product strategy.
